nodes.name: Token Identity Theory: Anomalous Monism
nodes.description: Donald Davidson's philosophical position that individual mental events (tokens) are identical to physical events, but mental event types don't correspond to physical event types.
key_ideas: Token identity: individual mental events = individual physical events
Type identity rejected: no strict laws correlating mental and physical types
Foundation for understanding NFT uniqueness vs fungibility
nodes.paper_ref: Nicolae Sfetcu — Token Identity Theory: Anomalous Monism (telework.ro)

nodes.name: Type vs Token Distinction
nodes.description: The philosophical distinction between types (categories/classes) and tokens (individual instances). In '100101', there are 6 tokens but only 2 types (0 and 1).
key_ideas: Token: single occurrence or instance
Type: category formed by identical tokens
Foundation for ERC-20 (type identity) vs ERC-721 (token identity)
nodes.paper_ref: Nicolae Sfetcu — Token Identity Theory: Anomalous Monism

nodes.name: Rationality and Holism Arguments
nodes.description: Davidson's arguments for the anomaly of the mental: beliefs require rational coherence and mental events presuppose other mental events.
key_ideas: Principle of Charity: interpreting beliefs as largely true and rational
Holism of the Mental: single mental events cannot be attributed in isolation
Mental events always presuppose other mental events

nodes.name: Fungibility Spectrum
nodes.description: The spectrum from fully fungible (interchangeable units) through semi-fungible (fungible within classes) to non-fungible (unique items).
key_ideas: Fungible: lumber, coins, loyalty points — fully interchangeable
Semi-fungible: tickets, clothing — interchangeable within class
Non-fungible: art, domains, real estate — unique items

nodes.name: ERC-20: Fungible Token Standard
nodes.description: Ethereum standard for fungible tokens where each token is identical and interchangeable. Data structure: address → balance mapping.
key_ideas: balanceOf(address) returns token balance
transferFrom(from, to, value) transfers tokens
All tokens are identical — type identity

nodes.name: ERC-721: Non-Fungible Token Standard
nodes.description: Ethereum standard for non-fungible tokens where each token has a unique tokenId. Data structure: tokenId → owner mapping.
key_ideas: ownerOf(tokenId) returns token owner
transferFrom(from, to, tokenId) transfers specific token
Each token is unique — token identity

nodes.name: ERC-1155: Semi-Fungible Token Standard
nodes.description: Ethereum standard combining fungible and non-fungible tokens. Data structure: id → address → quantity mapping.
key_ideas: balanceOf(address, id) returns quantity of specific token type
Supports both fungible and non-fungible in single contract
Efficient for gaming items: 20 identical swords as one id

nodes.name: Legitimacy: Higher-Order Acceptance
nodes.description: Vitalik Buterin's concept that legitimacy is a pattern of higher-order acceptance — people accept an outcome because they expect others to accept it too.
key_ideas: Legitimacy = pattern of higher-order acceptance
People play their part expecting others to do the same
Controls millions of dollars in value beyond code or keys
nodes.paper_ref: Vitalik Buterin — The Most Important Scarce Resource is Legitimacy (2021)

nodes.name: Coordination Games
nodes.description: Games where players benefit from coordinating on the same choice. Legitimacy matters because it determines which equilibrium everyone expects others to follow.
key_ideas: Example: which side of the road to drive on
Switching alone is catastrophic; switching together can be beneficial
Legitimacy determines the focal point in coordination
nodes.paper_ref: Vitalik Buterin — The Most Important Scarce Resource is Legitimacy (2021)

nodes.name: Sources of Legitimacy
nodes.description: Six ways legitimacy can be achieved: brute force, continuity, fairness, process, performance, and participation.
key_ideas: Brute force, Continuity (tradition), Fairness
Process (agreed procedures), Performance (good outcomes)
Participation (ownership through involvement)
nodes.paper_ref: Vitalik Buterin — The Most Important Scarce Resource is Legitimacy (2021)

nodes.name: Tokens as Social Contracts
nodes.description: Naval Ravikant's insight that tokens are backed by social contracts from creators and surrounding communities, not just code.
key_ideas: Token value from creator commitment + community acceptance
NFT ownership is programmable, verifiable, composable
Public blockchains as title registries for valuable things

nodes.name: Community-Driven NFT Value
nodes.description: NFT value rises and falls with community health. NFTs are monetized memes — their value reflects community belief and engagement.
key_ideas: Dying community = bleeding NFT value
Surging community = gaining NFT value
NFTs as monetized memes backed by social consensus

nodes.name: NFT Interoperability
nodes.description: NFTs following standards like ERC-721 are interoperable across wallets, marketplaces, and applications without custom integration.
key_ideas: Standardized interfaces enable universal compatibility
Same NFT usable across OpenSea, games, DeFi protocols
Composability: NFTs as building blocks

nodes.name: DAO vs Traditional Company
nodes.description: Comparison of DAOs (smart contracts, tokens, community governance) vs traditional companies (Delaware corp, VCs, executive governance).
key_ideas: Formation: smart contracts vs incorporation
Liquidity: Day 1 token vs 3-10 year exit
Governance: tokenholders vs executives/board

nodes.name: NFT + DeFi Fusion
nodes.description: Integration of NFTs with DeFi protocols: using NFTs as collateral, fractional ownership, and yield-generating NFT characters like Aavegotchi.
key_ideas: NFT as collateral for loans (NFTfi)
Fractional NFT ownership (NIFTEX)
Aavegotchi: NFT characters backed by DeFi collateral

nodes.name: Web Evolution: Web1 to Web3
nodes.description: The evolution from Web1 (open protocols, limited features) through Web2 (corporate platforms, advanced features) to Web3 (blockchain, community governance, value to participants).
key_ideas: Web1: community governance, limited features, no value capture
Web2: corporate governance, advanced features, value to big tech
Web3: community governance, advanced features, value to participants

nodes.name: Platform Take Rates: Web2 vs Web3
nodes.description: Comparison of value extraction between Web2 platforms (30%+ take rates) and Web3 protocols (0.06-2.5% take rates).
key_ideas: Web2: YouTube 30%, App Store 30%, Steam 45%
Web3: OpenSea 2.5%, Uniswap 0.3%, Ethereum 0.06%
Web3 returns more value to creators and users

nodes.name: NFT Market Cycles and Trends
nodes.description: Analysis of NFT market evolution from 2021 hype through 2022-2023 crash to 2026 maturation with utility-focused applications.
key_ideas: Price-innovation cycles: price → interest → ideas → startups
2021 peak → 2022-2023 crash → K-shaped recovery
Shift from speculation to utility (gaming, membership, credentials)

nodes.name: NFT Creator Royalties Challenge
nodes.description: The challenge of enforcing creator royalties on-chain. $1.9B+ paid to creators by 2023, but enforcement mechanisms remain contentious.
key_ideas: $1.9B+ in royalties paid to creators (2021-2023)
On-chain enforcement is technically difficult
Marketplace competition drove royalties toward zero

edges.description: Token identity theory builds on the type/token distinction from philosophy
Davidson's arguments explain why type identity fails for mental events
Type/token distinction maps directly to fungible/non-fungible tokens
ERC-20 implements the fungible end of the spectrum
ERC-721 implements the non-fungible end of the spectrum
ERC-1155 implements the middle of the fungibility spectrum
ERC-1155 combines ERC-20 fungibility with ERC-721 uniqueness
ERC-1155 combines ERC-721 uniqueness with ERC-20 fungibility
Legitimacy matters because social contexts are coordination games
Multiple sources contribute to establishing legitimacy
Token value derives from legitimacy-based social contracts
Community acceptance is what makes social contracts valuable
Standard interfaces enable cross-platform NFT interoperability
Community-driven value model enables DAO governance structures
NFT standards enable integration with DeFi protocols
Take rates illustrate the value distribution difference between web eras
Interoperability enables vibrant NFT markets and trading
Creator royalties depend on community acceptance and enforcement
Market dynamics directly impact royalty enforcement and adoption
